title: Parliament took up a Bill to widen Value Added Tax coverage, cut thresholds, tax cross‑border digital services, and raise the rate on financial services to 20.5%.
body_paragraphs: The draft law reduced the compulsory registration floor to Rs. 36 million a year and Rs. 9 million per taxable period, pulling many small and medium traders into the net compared with the current Rs. 60 million and Rs. 15 million levels. It also brought non‑resident online platforms into the regime from July 2026 while excluding business‑to‑business supplies that can be evidenced.

Banks and finance companies faced a higher levy on fee‑based activity under the special mechanism for the sector, taking the combined impost to 20.5%. New compliance tools included a mandate for secured point‑of‑sale systems and stiffer penalties, with maximum fines lifted to Rs. 1 million and imprisonment possible for false refund claims.

The revenue authority has been tasked with meeting a Rs. 2,402 billion collection target for 2026 after exceeding its 2025 goal by Rs. 42 billion. The Bill sits alongside joint audits with Customs and the rollout of some 12 million Taxpayer Identification Numbers (TINs) to broaden the file.

title: The Asian Development Bank signalled a strategic shift and said budget financing for Sri Lanka was under internal consideration.
body_paragraphs: Its President outlined at the 59th Annual Meeting in Samarkand that support would be framed around energy security, food systems and fiscal stability, with no figures disclosed. He also announced a US$ 70 billion programme to strengthen regional power grids and digital links by 2035, targeting about 20 gigawatts of cross‑border renewables.

A new joint initiative with Japan, ACCEL (Action for Creating Energy Security for Long‑term Resilience), was launched to help small and medium‑size firms (SMEs, smaller businesses with limited access to finance) cope with high fuel costs while scaling investment in efficiency and clean power. The lender said growth and inflation paths across developing Asia had worsened amid Middle East conflict, and it would use fast‑disbursing instruments and guarantees more flexibly.
bold_phrases: ACCEL (Action for Creating Energy Security for Long‑term Resilience)

title: Colombo consumer inflation accelerated to 5.4% year‑on‑year in April, breaching the Central Bank’s 5% point target.
body_paragraphs: The CCPI (Colombo Consumer Price Index, a monthly price basket for the capital) recorded a 2.99% month‑on‑month increase, driven mainly by transport costs linked to higher energy. Food prices rose 2.8% on an annual basis after near‑zero readings in February and March, while non‑food items climbed 6.8%.

The Central Bank said the earlier low inflation provided space to absorb energy pass‑through and that the medium‑term outlook remained around the target band of 3% to 7% if policies held. It warned the path was uncertain given the Middle East war’s impact on oil and freight, and that rates would respond if expectations de‑anchored.

title: Sri Lanka and the Maldives exchanged seven MoUs, expanding cooperation in defence, education, tourism, health and archives.
body_paragraphs: The agreements followed a State visit by President Mohamed Muizzu, with both sides also discussing a Bank of Maldives presence and premium fisheries exports into Sri Lanka. Leaders described the relationship as entering a new phase, with reciprocal land allocation for High Commissions and a business forum to pursue investment.

Shared Indian Ocean security concerns featured alongside plans for youth and sports exchanges and defence education links with General Sir John Kotelawala Defence University. Colombo said the Maldives remained its third‑largest SAARC export market and highlighted scope in boat‑building and twin‑destination tourism.

title: The Bar Association of Sri Lanka raised concern over a presidential remark referring to an upcoming court decision.
body_paragraphs: The lawyers’ body said judicial independence is an intangible constitutional heritage and cited Article 111C, which makes interference an offence. It added that public confidence could be eroded if statements suggested prior knowledge of verdicts and pledged to act to safeguard the rule of law.

title: A group of 26 Opposition MPs accused the President of undermining judicial independence in a joint statement.
body_paragraphs: They objected to a May Day reference inviting applause for a verdict expected on 25 May, saying it implied prior awareness. The lawmakers argued that justice must be seen to be done and urged a withdrawal of the comment.
bold_phrases: justice must be seen to be done

title: Nations Trust Bank completed the transfer of HSBC Sri Lanka’s retail banking business effective 1 May 2026.
body_paragraphs: About 200,000 customer accounts moved, covering deposits, cards, loans and a high‑net‑worth segment joining Nations Trust Bank Private Banking. The footprint expanded to 96 branches, with seven former HSBC sites in Colombo designated as private banking centres and others in Nugegoda, Jaffna and Kandy.

The bank said multiple channels were kept open during the migration, including extended weekend support and a 24‑hour help desk, as well as a dedicated microsite for FAQs. Staff of the acquired business were onboarded to ensure continuity.

title: Police arrested more than 120 foreign nationals in Rajagiriya over alleged financial fraud operations.
body_paragraphs: The suspects, from several Asian and African countries, were detained across multiple floors of an apartment complex where devices including laptops and phones were seized. Forensic analysis was ordered after some individuals fled during the raid.

title: Cabinet cleared rules empowering the Central Bank to collect statistics with administrative sanctions for non‑compliance.
body_paragraphs: The framework under the 2023 Central Bank Act set formats, confidentiality provisions and penalty powers, and will be tabled in Parliament. Ministers also approved submitting the Bank’s 2025 audited financial statements and operational report to the legislature.

title: Parliament’s finance committee heard that a new insolvency law could unlock over Rs. 30 billion in distressed assets.
body_paragraphs: Witnesses said a rescue‑first regime would allow time‑bound creditor‑approved restructurings instead of near‑automatic liquidations, easing bank balance sheets and attracting special‑situation capital. Stakeholders flagged tax clarity and court capacity as execution risks and urged alignment with company law and future limited liability partnership rules.

title: Menzies Aviation won a three‑year ground handling contract for SriLankan Airlines in Sydney worth AUD 8.32 million.
body_paragraphs: The award followed limited international bidding after the previous agreement expired on 30 April 2026, and starts from 1 May. The Cabinet spokesman said the company was the substantially responsive lowest bidder recommended by the standing committee.

title: Sri Lanka sent its largest‑ever delegation to the SelectUSA summit, with 14 firms pursuing US‑linked investment.
body_paragraphs: The US Embassy said the group was building networks across healthcare, manufacturing and digital services to deepen bilateral commercial ties. Organisers aim to connect foreign investors with state and regional agencies for site selection and incentives.

title: Russia’s Health Minister arrived in Colombo for a three‑day visit for talks on pharmaceuticals, training and hospital links.
body_paragraphs: The programme included meetings with the Health and Mass Media Minister and the Foreign Affairs Minister, and visits to the National Hospital and the University of Colombo’s Faculty of Medicine. Both sides planned to explore academic exchanges and cooperation in Ayurveda, health tourism and specialist training.
